comorbidities.name: Diabetic distal symmetrical sensorimotor polyneuropathy with loss of protective sensation

pathophysiology_summary: Fifteen years of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes has produced advanced distal sensorimotor polyneuropathy with complete loss of protective sensation. Repetitive, unfelt pressure under the first metatarsal head (a high-pressure weight-bearing point, exaggerated by Charcot deformity) drives callus formation and eventual full-thickness ulceration. Because the foot is insensate the patient neither feels nor guards the wound, so silent trauma continues and the ulcer deepens. Hyperglycaemia impairs neutrophil function and microvascular healing, allowing bacterial invasion — the malodorous seropurulent discharge and 1 cm perilesional erythema indicate established local infection, and the wound depth raises the possibility of deep-space infection and contiguous osteomyelitis. Bounding pedal pulses and a warm, dry foot indicate preserved macrovascular perfusion with autonomic neuropathy (dry skin) — this is a neuropathic, not ischaemic, ulcer. The defining danger is that the classic warning signal (pain) is absent and the patient is systemically well, so the limb-threatening severity is easily under-appreciated. Telehealth cannot probe wound depth, image bone, culture the wound or debride — definitive assessment requires urgent in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: Painless deep plantar ulcer with malodorous discharge and surrounding callus in a long-standing diabetic — the classic neuropathic ulcer pattern with local infection.
A deep diabetic foot ulcer with infection carries a high risk of contiguous bone infection; cannot be excluded on history or video and mandates probe-to-bone and imaging.
Perilesional erythema raises the possibility of ascending cellulitis, which would escalate urgency.
Must be considered in any diabetic foot ulcer, but the painlessness, warmth and (sealed) bounding pulses argue against ischaemia.
Loss of protective sensation confirmed, local infection confirmed, systemic sepsis screen currently negative — established locally infected neuropathic ulcer.
Depth and chronicity keep osteomyelitis live; remains unexcludable without probe-to-bone and X-ray — drives the in-person escalation.
Currently screened negative (afebrile, well, not confused) but must be actively safety-netted — diabetic foot infection can decompensate silently.
Painless, warm foot with bounding pulses (sealed exam) is inconsistent with ischaemic ulceration, which is typically painful with cool, pulseless skin.
Plantar weight-bearing location and absence of gaiter-area oedema/haemosiderin argue against a venous ulcer.
The pressure-point location under thick callus in a severely neuropathic diabetic points to mechanical/neuropathic aetiology; malignant change is not the working diagnosis for an acute presentation.

Limb-threatening infected neuropathic ulcer; deep infection and osteomyelitis cannot be excluded remotely — requires urgent same-day in-person assessment (probe-to-bone, X-ray, swab, antibiotics, debridement, offloading).
The specific unexcludable diagnosis that makes in-person imaging/probing mandatory today rather than routine wound care.

red_flags.description: Deep full-thickness plantar ulcer that may probe to bone — risk of osteomyelitis / deep-space infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Depth and possible bone involvement change management from wound care to urgent imaging, probing and possibly IV antibiotics/surgical debridement — cannot be assessed via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undiagnosed osteomyelitis progresses to bone destruction and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Established local wound infection — malodorous seropurulent discharge with perilesional erythema
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms an infected (not merely colonised) diabetic foot ulcer requiring prompt antibiotics and in-person wound assessment.

red_flags.description: Complete loss of protective sensation with continued weight-bearing on an insensate foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: The painlessness removes the natural warning system; silent ongoing trauma and delayed presentation make this a high-risk limb, and the absence of pain must not be read as reassurance.

red_flags.description: Systemic sepsis features — fever, rigors, confusion, tachycardia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently absent (patient afebrile and well), but a diabetic foot infection can progress to systemic sepsis; screening establishes the safety baseline and defines the escalation trigger.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading erythema up the foot/leg or black/dusky (necrotic) tissue
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal ascending cellulitis or necrotising/wet gangrene — an immediate surgical emergency; not present now but is the key deterioration sign to safety-net.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading 'it doesn't hurt at all' as evidence the wound is minor. In a neuropathic diabetic foot the absence of pain is itself the danger — it means loss of protective sensation, not a benign lesion.
Being reassured that the patient is comfortable, afebrile and 'completely unbothered' and therefore under-triaging a limb-threatening infection.
Attempting to coach the family through home dressing changes and antiseptics instead of escalating for the in-person probing, imaging and debridement that a diabetic foot infection requires.
Settling on 'infected ulcer, give oral antibiotics' without recognising that deep infection and osteomyelitis cannot be excluded remotely and demand urgent in-person workup.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Probe-to-bone test and true wound depth
Plain X-ray of the foot to assess for osteomyelitis and Charcot changes
Palpation of pedal pulses / ankle-brachial assessment of perfusion
10 g monofilament and neurological examination confirming loss of protective sensation
Wound swab / deep tissue culture and sensitivities
Bloods — FBC, CRP, HbA1c, renal function
Reliability of the home thermometer reading and true vital signs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment at an emergency department or high-risk foot service today for wound probing, X-ray to exclude osteomyelitis, wound swab, commencement of empirical antibiotics and debridement; strict offloading and no barefoot walking in the interim; call 000 / attend ED immediately if systemic sepsis or spreading/necrotic signs develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the family and recommending home wound care (or a routine GP appointment in days) because the patient is painless and systemically well risks missed osteomyelitis, uncontrolled deep infection and limb loss.

clinician_review_notes: Deliberate under-triage-resistance case. The presentation is engineered to look non-urgent (no pain, patient comfortable and afebrile, discovered incidentally) while being genuinely limb-threatening. A correct telehealth answer recognises the limb threat and secures urgent same-day in-person assessment (T4) with strict offloading and sepsis safety-netting; it does NOT default to 000/ambulance (patient is systemically well — that is over-triage) and does NOT settle for home wound care or a routine GP appointment (under-triage). Candidate codes are unverified pending terminology receipt.
